title: MONGOOSE: A Learnable LSH Framework for Efficient Neural Network Training

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## MONGOOSE: A LEARNABLE LSH FRAMEWORK FOR EFFICIENT NEURAL NETWORK TRAINING

**Beidi Chen**[1] **, Zichang Liu**[2] **, Binghui Peng**[3] **, Zhaozhuo Xu**[2] **, Jonathan Lingjie Li**[1] **, Tri Dao**[1] **, Zhao Song**[4] **, Anshumali Shrivastava**[2] **, Christopher Ré**[1]

1 Stanford University, 2 Rice University, 3 Columbia University, 4 Princeton University

{beidic,jlli,trid,chrismre}@stanford.edu, bp2601@columbia.edu {zl71,zx22,anshumali}@rice.edu, zhaos@princeton.edu

## ABSTRACT

Recent advances by practitioners in the deep learning community have breathed new life into Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH), using it to reduce memory and time bottlenecks in neural network (NN) training. However, while LSH has sublinear guarantees for approximate near-neighbor search in theory, it is known to have inefficient query time in practice due to its use of random hash functions. Moreover, when model parameters are changing, LSH suffers from update overhead. This work is motivated by an observation that model parameters evolve slowly, such that the changes do not always require an LSH update to maintain performance. This phenomenon points to the potential for a reduction in update time and allows for a modified learnable version of data-dependent LSH to improve query time at a low cost. We use the above insights to build MONGOOSE, an end-to-end LSH framework for efficient NN training. In particular, MONGOOSE is equipped with a scheduling algorithm to adaptively perform LSH updates with provable guarantees and learnable hash functions to improve query efficiency. Empirically, we validate MONGOOSE on large-scale deep learning models for recommendation systems and language modeling. We find that it achieves up to 8% better accuracy compared to previous LSH approaches, with 6 _._ 5 _×_ speed-up and 6 _×_ reduction in memory usage.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Locality Sensitive Hashing (LSH) has been adapted to address the computational and memory bottlenecks of large-scale neural network (NN) training in natural language processing (Chandar et al., 2016; Rae et al., 2016; Kitaev et al., 2020), computer vision (Chen et al., 2015) and recommendation systems (Spring & Shrivastava, 2017; Chen et al., 2020). Specifically, giant matrix multiplications in linear layers preceding a softmax can be approximated using nearest neighbor search (NNS) techniques, which often rely on LSH. However, LSH methods used in NNs are inefficient. Although LSH achieves sub-linear query time in theory, it is known to suffer from high query and pre-processing (update) overhead in practice (Erik et al., 2018). In the setting of NN training, where data points for LSH are model parameters, this overhead is exacerbated by a high number of updates due to constantly evolving model parameters.

The most established solution for reducing LSH query overhead is data-dependent or learningbased hashing, which uses adaptive hash functions to optimize the LSH bucket distribution for each dataset (Andoni & Razenshteyn, 2015; Dong et al., 2019). These methods reduce query time by incurring a one-time offline cost to learn useful data input patterns in a preprocessing step. The learning techniques are often computationally complex, but can lead to a net reduction in overall query time. However, in NN training, the expensive preprocessing procedure has to be repeated each time the parameters are updated. Naïvely applying these techniques would increase the LSH update overhead rather than reduce it. A more appropriate data-dependent LSH framework would ideally (i) have a deeper understanding of the training dynamics of model parameters, a setting in which LSH has not been well-studied, (ii) be able to perform low-cost updates to account for evolving parameters, and (iii) have better query time while accurately approximating matrix multiplication.
We argue that it is unnecessary to view evolving model parameters as streaming data and not every parameter update requires an LSH update. In fact, LSH updates are necessary only when the NN gra-

dient steps are large enough to cause the model parameters’ LSH hash codes to change. Therefore, we count the number of hash code changes that occur for important models such as Transformers and fully-connected NNs from previous work (Chen et al., 2020; Kitaev et al., 2020). We find that only 1% of the hash codes change after each epoch on average for Transformers, and 5% for a fullyconnected NNs, so most weights do not change on a scale that is enough to trigger an LSH update. Furthermore, the rate of hash code change initially decays exponentially and eventually plateaus, as shown later in Figure 3. We calculate that a **100** _×_ speed up is possible with an update oracle. In contrast, current approaches with LSH have not fully exploited this observation. We show in Section 4.2 that they either blindly skip updates for speed, resulting in a 10-point accuracy drop, or suffer from a 20 _×_ slow-down.

We demonstrate that these slowly changing hash codes provide two opportunities to realize the ideal data-dependent LSH framework described above (shown as the intuition for the scheduler in Figure 1). First, given weight changes, an algorithm could adaptively schedule LSH updates to realize low update overhead. Second, given current weights, we could learn data-dependent hash functions for better LSH bucket distribution to achieve fast query time. However, this comes with three challenges: (i) how to characterize the slowly-changing phenomenon without computing hash codes, (ii) how to schedule the updates without the oracle, and (iii) how to learn data-dependent hash functions for shorter query time without compromising on the update time.

In this paper, we first provide a general formulation of the problem as _dynamic NNS_ in Section 2. In Section 3, we propose MONGOOSE, a framework for fast and memory-efficient NN training to address the above challenges. We show one major observation, _slow change_ , and two key components based on it in MONGOOSE. Specifically,

- In Section 3.1, we measure the _ℓ_ 2 norm of the weight changes and their hash code changes during training on a fully-connected NN. (More models are studied in Appendix A). We find that (i) the hash codes are slowly changing (ii) both quantities share similar trajectories, but the former’s slow change is the necessary condition of the latter’s. Therefore, we formally define the slow change of _ℓ_ 2 norm of weight changes in Assumption 3.1, which is the key to build MONGOOSE.

- In Section 3.2, we present an algorithm for scheduling efficient LSH updates. We closely analyze our scheduler’s theoretical guarantees and provide an upper bound of its running time, which is proportional to the _ℓ_ 2 norm of the weight changes. Therefore, under the _slow change_ assumption, we show that our scheduler is provably faster than previous approaches.

- In Section 3.3, we propose a method of learning parameterized LSH hash functions (e.g., SimHash (Charikar, 2002)) during NN training. Our method utilizes intermediate activations during the forward pass to generate training inputs for tuning LSH hash functions with little overhead. Combining this with our scheduler further decreases the overhead, while providing hash functions that better separate data.

Finally, in Section 4, we demonstrate the efficacy of MONGOOSE on two LSH-NN systems, SLIDE and Reformer. For SLIDE applications, we show up to 6 _._ 5 _×_ speedup in time and 8% higher accuracy on three datasets. For Reformer applications, we show improvement in perplexity when training lan-

## 3 MONGOOSE: A FRAMEWORK FOR LEARNABLE LSH

We present the workflow of our main framework in Figure 1. In Section 3.1, we first show the key observation that encourages the design of MONGOOSE and formally define the _slow change_ assumption. In Section 3.2, we introduce our first component, an algorithm for scheduling LSH updates with provable guarantees based on the assumption. Finally, we present the second component, lowcost learnable LSH hash functions, in Section 3.3. More details about the efficient implementation of MONGOOSE are in Appendix F.

## 3.2 A SMART SCHEDULER FOR LEARNABLE LSH UPDATES

In this section, we introduce a dynamic data structure to schedule updates for LSH. Our scheduler provides significant speedup under two realistic assumptions without compromising the theoretical guarantee of LSH: (1) _slow change_ defined in Assumption 3.1, (2) _batch speed up_ defined in Assumption 3.2. The scheduler can generalize to any near-neighbor search data structure (NNS-ds), but we show a design for LSH as an example since that is the NNS-ds in MONGOOSE.

Our scheduler is inspired by a dynamic data structure proposed in a recent line of work (Cohen et al., 2019), which was originally designed for inverse maintainance in Linear Programming solvers. We provide a novel application here for the dynamic LSH problem.

We use _S_ to denote the set of indices where _w_ and _v_ differ significantly (see Line 20). LSH.UPDATE (see line 17) takes indices of the neural weights and updates the LSH hashcode correspondingly.

**Overview of algorithm:** During the training process, we maintain two copies of the NN weights _w, v_ , where _w_ are the actual weights that are updated via gradient descent and _v_ approximately equals _w_ except for a few coordinates. We set an empirical threshold of _ϵ_ mds. Due to the robustness of the NNS problem, we can tolerate this small error _ϵ_ mds such that only those coordinates with a difference greater than _ϵ_ mds matter. To exploit the benefit of batch updating, we perform lazy updates on the LSH hash table. In particular, we update the LSH hash table if and only if the difference between _w, v_ exceeds some threshold number _n[ρ]_ (see line 9 to line 11). One key part of our update procedure is that we also update some low-error coordinates when there are _too many_ of them (see line 13 to line 16). This procedure can be seen as forecasting future updates. The intuition is that when there are too many coordinates close to the decision boundary, they are likely to change within a short amount of time. Updating these all together could further take advantage of the batch updates. To answer an LSH query, we keep track of a short list _S_ (see line 20), which records coordinates that differ significantly between _w, v_ . We can interpret _S_ as the indices of corrupted datapoints to keep track of when performing an LSH query, since they are no longer the same. In the query procedure (see line 22 to line 24), besides performing the normal LSH query, we also check _S_ and pick up weights that are close to _x_ . Hence, we balance the overhead of query time with update time, where update time is usually the bottleneck.

## 3.3 HASH FUNCTION LEARNING

In this section, we provide a simple streaming algorithm to learn parameterized hash functions. Here, the learning objective is to track the weight distribution as the weights change to better separate the

input data to the NNS problem. Recall that in last section, we show an algorithm for scheduling the LSH updates, which makes it possible to involve learning for LSH and improve the query efficiency. Therefore, the overall update overhead can be reduced even with learning overhead if scheduled well, since the update time is closely related to query time.

## 3.3.1 LEARNABLE LSH

We view the sequence of neural network weights throughout training as a matrix stream. In each training iteration, if our scheduler schedules an update, we compute a training signal with which we can train our LSH parameters. Specifically, we compute the classical triplet loss (Chechik et al., 2010) using positive and negative samples from the current model weights for each LSH query. Here, positive samples (denoted by _P_ +) are neurons that have higher inner product within the input retrieved by LSH, while negative samples (denoted by _P−_ ) are neurons with lower inner product that are selected. Denote a set of neurons in a particular layer as _C_ = _{vr |_ 0 _≤ r < m}_ and the input embedding as _qi_ . Denote the set of

## 3.3.2 FURTHER SPEEDUP

We demonstrate how to optimize learnable LSH for further acceleration in NN training. Here, we provide the main insights; further details are in Appendix A.
**Layer Selection:** In previous LSH-NN methods such as SLIDE or Reformer, the designation of whether a layer uses LSH is treated as a tunable hyperparameter. For example, Reformer applies LSH in

Figure 4: Attention distribution examples in different layers: Y-axis is the least number of attentions summing to 90% of full attentions. The annotations represent the median. The total number of attentions (or sequence length) is 512.

three out of twelve attention layers in their final model based on empirical performance. In MONGOOSE we make this designation in a more principled manner. The intuition behind the design can be presented by visualizing the statistics of attention weights in different layers of the Transformer model (Figure 4). The three plots represent the distribution of the average number of coordinates with “heavy” attention for each layer. We also calculate the size of the minimal set of attention weights that captures 90% of the total attention ((Ramsauer et al., 2020) perform a similar experiment). The median for the left plot is 2, meaning that they are well-separated and LSH can perform well on such a distribution. On the other hand, learnable LSH can be very helpful for the right plot, because classical LSH is inefficient for this distribution. Therefore, we can use this observation when deciding between LSH or learnable LSH in each layer for later training stages or fine-tuning on downstream tasks.

From a system design perspective, MONGOOSE improves the flexibility of LSH’s usage in NN training. We provide a smart scheduler to decide when to perform LSH updates and visualization on when to use LSH or learnable LSH. Therefore, the training process has the following tunable parameters for LSH: (i) the layers which we apply random LSH to, (ii) the layers for which we can use learning to improve LSH’s random hash functions, (iii) the runtime that LSH or learnable LSH should perform the update in. Setting these parameters appropriately will enable much more efficient NN training or fine-tuning, as supported by our theory and observations.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In Section 4.1 we present the results of experiments that demonstrate the higher performance of MONGOOSE over other LSH-NN frameworks during training. Then we study the effectiveness of the two components: (i) smart scheduler in Section 4.2, (ii) learnable LSH in Section 4.3[1] .

## 4.1 MAIN RESULTS: MONGOOSE IN NN TRAINING

We present the results of main experiments that demonstrate MONGOOSE’s advantage in end-to-end NN training over two different LSH-NN baselines on recommendation and language modeling tasks.

## 4.1.1 TASK1: EXTREME MULTI-LABEL CLASSIFICATION

**Settings:** We compare the performance of MONGOOSE against SLIDE and networks without LSH (Full-Computation) on 3 large-scale multi-label classification datasets: Wiki10-31k, Delicious200k, Wiki-325k and Amz-670k. The datasets are obtained from the Extreme Classification Repository (Bhatia et al., 2016), which is a benchmark for various recommendation systems. The model is a fully-connected network with one hidden-layer. The statistics of each dataset are in Appendix D. ## 4.1.2 TASK2: EFFICIENT TRANSFORMER

**Settings:** We run experiments on two tasks from Reformer (Kitaev et al., 2020). The first task is a synthetic sequence duplication task where inputs are of the form 0 _w_ 0 _w_ and _w ∈{_ 0 _, ..., N }[∗]_ . This task is useful for demonstrating the effectiveness of LSH attention: it requires non-local attention lookups and therefore cannot be solved by any model relying on sparse attention with a limited range ( _e.g._ , local attention). We compare MONGOOSE with a 1-layer Reformer by replacing the dataindependent LSH in the attention layer with learnable LSH, updating the LSH parameters according to our smart scheduler. The second task is a standard language modeling task on the enwik8 dataset. For this second task, we use sequence length of 8192 and 10 LSH-attention layers.

**Results:** We compare the test loss of MONGOOSE and Reformer on each task. The loss curve for the synthetic sequence duplication task is shown in Figure 5 (right). On this task, MONGOOSE achieves a loss value of 0.75, which is 0.5 better than Reformer. Moreover, MONGOOSE only needs 20% time to reach the same loss as Reformer (5 _×_ speed-up). Similarly, on enwik8, Reformer only reaches 2.65 in perplexity, while MONGOOSE achieves 2.59 in the same setting. Both MONGOOSE and Reformer (with LSH) save 6 _×_ memory over the original Transformer in above two tasks (more details are in Appendix D.4).

## **Effect of Smart Scheduler:** The first approach

(“Infrequent Scheduler”) performs an update on a fixed schedule, every 1000 iterations (6 times per epoch on Wiki-325K). The other baseline performs an update every iteration (“W/O Scheduler”). We compare speed (number of batches processed per second) and accuracy ( _P_ @1 and _P_ @5) of these schedulers in Table 3. On average, MONGOOSE Scheduler reduces more than **97** % of the update overhead compared to W/O Scheduler, leading an end-to-end 20 _×_ speed-up. Meanwhile, although Infrequent Scheduler is faster in speed, it fails to reach the same task accuracy as the other approaches. In fact, MONGOOSE Scheduler achieves Infrequent Scheduler’s final accuracy in **66** % less time. We can see that MONGOOSE substantially reduces LSH update overhead while maintaining performance.

## 4.3 STUDY ON LEARNABLE LSH

**Effect of Learnable LSH:** In this section, we compare the NNS performance of MONGOOSE’s learnable LSH scheme to classical LSH on their NNS ability using the same number of hash functions. In Figure 6 (left) we plot the difference between average inner products computed using neurons retrieved by learnable LSH (MONGOOSE) and random LSH (SLIDE) and inner products using the same number of randomly chosen neurons. A negative value on these plots indicates that LSH is performing worse than random sampling. We can see that at the beginning of training, when weights are initialized randomly, data-independent LSH is sufficient to partition the data. However, as training progresses, SimHash used in SLIDE fails to adapt to the evolving weight distribution, while our selection better approximates the full softmax.

## 5 CONCLUSION

In this work, we make a novel observation on the slowly changing LSH hash codes during LSHNN training. Based on the observation, we present MONGOOSE, a learnable LSH framework that outperforms original LSH methods in efficient neural network training. We demonstrate two key designs in the framework. One is a smart scheduling algorithm which reduces the LSH update overhead. The other one is a low cost learnable LSH which can improve the query efficiency and hashing quality. We empirically verify the effectiveness of our framework on both recommendation and language modeling tasks. The slowly changing observation along with the smart scheduler in MONGOOSE opens up the opportunity of designing new NNS-ds for dynamic similarity search for efficient NN training.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces MONGOOSE, a framework for accelerating neural network training by combining learnable LSH hash functions with a smart scheduling algorithm that exploits the 'slow change' of model weights.

The paper provides a compelling approach to reducing the computational overhead of LSH in neural network training. The empirical results on extreme multi-label classification and Transformer-based language modeling are strong and show clear speedup gains. However, the theoretical justification for the learnable hash functions and the sensitivity of the hyperparameter tuning for the scheduler require more rigorous treatment.

Overview issues:

- Lack of Theoretical Guarantees for Learnable Hash Functions: While the paper provides theoretical analysis for the LSH maintenance scheduler in Section 3.2, the learnable hash function component described in Section 3.3 lacks equivalent theoretical grounding. The transition from random LSH to learnable LSH is justified empirically, but the paper does not define the conditions under which the learned hash functions maintain the (c1, c2)-accuracy guarantees established in Definition 2.1. Without a formal characterization of the optimization objective (Equation 3) relative to the LSH approximation error, it remains unclear if the performance gains are generalizable or specific to the chosen benchmarks. I suggest adding a discussion or a simplified proof sketch regarding the stability of the learned hash functions under the proposed triplet loss.

- Sensitivity and Overhead of the Scheduler Hyperparameters: The scheduler relies on the precision parameter epsilon_mds (Section 3.2.1), which is described as a 'sufficiently small precision parameter.' The paper does not provide a systematic analysis of how the performance of the scheduler scales with this parameter across different model architectures or training phases. If epsilon_mds requires careful tuning for every new dataset, the claim that the scheduler is 'oblivious' to the exact form of update time is weakened. A sensitivity analysis showing the robustness of the scheduler to varying epsilon_mds values would significantly strengthen the practical utility of the framework.

- Unclear Generalization Beyond Extreme Classification: The primary empirical validation focuses on extreme multi-label classification tasks and specific Transformer attention layers. The 'slow change' assumption (Assumption 3.1) is well-supported for these tasks, but it is not obvious that this phenomenon holds for all layers in deep, non-linear architectures or during the initial phases of training where gradients are highly volatile. The paper should explicitly discuss the limitations of the slow-change assumption, particularly in early training or when using aggressive learning rate schedules, to help practitioners understand when the MONGOOSE framework might fail.

- Memory Overhead of Hash Table Updates: The paper claims a 6x reduction in memory usage, but the memory overhead of storing the learnable hash function parameters and the auxiliary structures required for the scheduler is not fully accounted for in the comparison. In Section 3.3, the REBUILD operation involves updating hash tables based on new hash functions, which may introduce temporary memory spikes. A more detailed breakdown of the memory footprint—specifically comparing the memory used for the LSH structures versus the model weights—would provide a more transparent view of the actual savings.

- Empirical validation of the 'slow change' assumption in early training: The paper relies on Assumption 3.1, which posits that weight changes are slow enough to justify infrequent LSH updates. However, the provided Figure 3 shows a sharp drop in $\Delta W$ and $\Delta H$ at the start of training, suggesting that the assumption is most violated during the initial phase where gradients are volatile. The paper lacks a concrete demonstration of how the MONGOOSE scheduler handles this high-volatility regime. I suggest adding a plot showing the LSH update frequency versus training iteration for a standard model like BERT-base, specifically highlighting the first 5-10% of training, to show whether the scheduler correctly defaults to more frequent updates or if it suffers from performance degradation during this critical period.

- Ablation of learnable hash function overhead vs. query time gains: The paper introduces a learnable LSH mechanism (Section 3.3) to improve bucket distribution, but does not isolate the computational cost of the triplet loss optimization from the query-time speedup. While the authors claim 'little overhead,' they do not provide a breakdown of the time spent in the REHASH/REBUILD operations compared to the time saved during the forward pass. To demonstrate that the learnable component is a net positive, the authors should report the wall-clock time spent on hash function updates per iteration and compare this directly to the time saved in the matrix multiplication step for a standard benchmark like Wiki-325K.

- Concrete example of the scheduler's behavior on a non-convex loss surface: The scheduler's theoretical guarantees in Theorem 3.3 are based on the L2 norm of weight changes, which is a proxy for hash code changes. However, the relationship between weight-space geometry and hash-space stability is not explicitly derived for non-convex neural network landscapes. A worked example using a simple, fully-specified two-layer MLP on a synthetic dataset—calculating the exact threshold at which the scheduler triggers an update and verifying it against the actual hash code change—would clarify the tightness of the bound in Assumption 3.1 and provide readers with a concrete calibration for the sensitivity parameter $\epsilon_{\text{mds}}$.

Recommendation: minor revision

Revision targets:

- Include a sensitivity analysis for the epsilon_mds hyperparameter to demonstrate the scheduler's robustness.

- Provide a more formal discussion or theoretical justification regarding how the learned hash functions preserve the (c1, c2)-accuracy guarantees.

- Add a section explicitly discussing the limitations of the 'slow change' assumption in early training stages or with high learning rates.

- Clarify the memory overhead of the auxiliary structures used for the scheduler and the REBUILD operation.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces MONGOOSE, a framework that combines a smart scheduler with learnable LSH to reduce update overhead during neural network training. The core observation that hash codes change slowly during training is empirically supported and forms a reasonable basis for adaptive updates. The theoretical formulation of dynamic NNS and the maintenance data structure show promise, and the empirical results on recommendation and language modeling tasks demonstrate tangible speed and memory gains.

The work addresses a practical bottleneck in LSH-based neural training by decoupling update frequency from every gradient step. The integration of a scheduling mechanism with data-dependent hash learning is a coherent design choice, and the reported speedups are compelling. The main weakness lies in the gap between the theoretical assumptions, the implementation details of the learning component, and the statistical rigor of the evaluation.

Overview issues:

- Non-differentiability of the hash learning objective: The paper introduces a triplet loss to optimize parameterized SimHash functions in Section 3.3 and Equation 3, but the sign operation inherent to SimHash is non-differentiable. The methodology does not specify which gradient approximation technique—straight-through estimator, continuous relaxation, or Gumbel-Softmax—is employed to backpropagate through the hash assignments. Without this detail, the learning mechanism is mathematically undefined and impossible to reproduce. Readers will naturally question how gradients flow through discrete bucket assignments during the REHASH step. The authors should explicitly state the gradient estimator used, justify its selection based on stability, and provide a sensitivity analysis showing how different estimators affect convergence speed and final accuracy.

- Fragility of the slow change assumption under non-stationary training dynamics: Assumption 3.1 bounds the expected movement and variance of neural network weights, forming the theoretical foundation for the smart scheduler in Theorem 3.3. This assumption holds during the plateau phase shown in Figure 3, but it breaks down during early training, learning rate warmups, or fine-tuning phases where parameter updates are inherently large. When the bound is violated, the scheduler's amortized time guarantee no longer applies, yet the paper provides no fallback strategy for these regimes. The theoretical claims implicitly assume a stationary data distribution, which contradicts the fundamental nature of stochastic gradient descent. The authors need to characterize the scheduler's behavior when $C_1$ and $C_2$ spike, and either incorporate a hard threshold for forced updates or explicitly restrict the theoretical analysis to the convergence phase of training.

- Missing quantification of approximation error propagation: The framework relies on approximate nearest neighbor queries to replace exact matrix multiplications, but the paper never traces how query approximation errors propagate through the backward pass to affect gradient fidelity and final model convergence. Definition 2.1 provides a static accuracy definition for the query operation, yet it does not connect $(c_1, c_2)$-accuracy to the expected error in weight updates or loss landscapes. The empirical results report accuracy gains, but they lack a controlled ablation that isolates the LSH approximation quality from the scheduler and learning components. To validate the practical utility of the method, the authors should include a trade-off curve plotting accuracy against query error bounds, and provide a theoretical or empirical analysis linking hash collision rates to gradient noise levels.

- Insufficient statistical reporting and hyperparameter sensitivity analysis: The experimental evaluation in Section 4 reports performance metrics from single runs without confidence intervals or multiple random seeds. Table 1 and Table 3 present point estimates for speedups and accuracy, making it impossible to assess whether the observed improvements exceed standard variance in deep learning training. Top-tier venues expect statistical robustness and sensitivity analyses for key hyperparameters like update frequency, number of hash tables, and the precision parameter $	heta_{	ext{mds}}$. Without this information, the claimed $6.5	imes$ speedup and $8	ext{	extpercnt}$ accuracy improvement lack statistical grounding. The revision must include mean and standard deviation over at least three independent seeds, along with a sensitivity table demonstrating how performance degrades or stabilizes when scheduler thresholds and hash learning rates are perturbed.

- Unquantified overhead of the maintenance data structure: Algorithm 1 and Theorem 3.3 claim an amortized update time proportional to $(C_1+C_2)	heta_{	ext{mds}}$, but the practical computational cost of maintaining the dual weight copies $w$ and $v$, computing element-wise differences, sorting deviation magnitudes, and managing the corruption set $S$ is never measured. The paper assumes batch speedup relies on hardware parallelism, yet it does not provide a flops or wall-clock breakdown of the scheduler itself versus the LSH hash table updates. If the maintenance overhead approaches or exceeds the savings from infrequent updates, the theoretical advantage collapses in practice. The authors should add a detailed timing breakdown of each scheduler step, compare the actual overhead against the theoretical bound, and include a scaling experiment showing how the scheduler performs across different batch sizes and sequence lengths.

- Heuristic layer selection lacks automation and generalization: Section 3.3.2 proposes using attention distribution statistics to decide which Transformer layers should use random versus learnable LSH, but this approach requires manual inspection of Figure 4 and does not generalize to other architectures like CNNs or MLPs. The method relies on a fixed threshold of attention weights summing to 90%, which is dataset-dependent and requires prior knowledge of the model's internal dynamics. A learnable framework should ideally automate this routing decision rather than depending on post-hoc visualization. The authors need to propose an automated criterion based on measurable quantities like attention entropy, gradient norms, or a lightweight proxy task. They should also discuss how the framework would adapt to architectures where attention is absent or replaced by other sparse mechanisms.

- Missing simulation to verify theoretical amortized update bounds: The paper derives an amortized update time bound in Theorem 3.3 but never tests it against a controlled synthetic distribution. Readers cannot verify whether the smart scheduler actually approaches the claimed scaling when weight updates follow known patterns. A simulation generating weight matrices with prescribed C1 and C2 values would ground the theory in practice. The authors should construct a synthetic DGP where weight updates are sampled from a Gaussian with controlled variance, track the scheduler's actual update frequency, and plot the empirical amortized time against the theoretical upper bound. Without this, the theoretical contribution remains unverified outside of idealized assumptions.

- Underdeveloped strategy for test-time deployment and fine-tuning: The framework is explicitly built for training dynamics, yet the paper completely omits how the learned LSH structure behaves during inference or fine-tuning. Practitioners will need to know whether the hash table requires rebuilding when the model is deployed, or how the scheduler adapts to distribution shifts on a new dataset. The authors should discuss a deployment protocol, such as freezing the hash functions after training and quantifying the accuracy drop if the hash table is not updated. They could also include an experiment showing how MONGOOSE performs when fine-tuning a pre-trained model on a downstream task, which would clarify the framework's practical utility beyond initial training.

- Missing comparison to modern efficient attention baselines: The experimental section only benchmarks against SLIDE and Reformer, both of which rely on fixed random LSH. The paper misses a direct comparison to contemporary learnable or kernel-based efficient attention methods like Performer, Linear Transformer, or Nyströmformer. These methods also aim to reduce quadratic complexity and represent the current standard for efficient training. Including them would demonstrate whether MONGOOSE's learnable LSH offers a distinct advantage over differentiable attention approximations. The authors should add a table reporting training throughput and final perplexity or accuracy alongside these modern baselines to properly position the method.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Explicitly define the gradient approximation method for the non-differentiable hash functions and add a sensitivity analysis across different estimators.

- Provide a statistical evaluation with multiple random seeds, confidence intervals, and a hyperparameter sensitivity table for scheduler thresholds and learning rates.

- Quantify the actual computational overhead of the maintenance data structure and compare it against the theoretical amortized bound using wall-clock measurements.

- Add an automated layer-selection criterion and discuss how the framework generalizes to architectures beyond Transformers and fully-connected networks.
